what: The machine every number in bench/ was measured on. A benchmark without provenance is an anecdote.

host.cpu: AMD Ryzen 5 7600X3D, 6 cores / 12 threads, 4.10 GHz base
host.raw_data_path: C:\data\tarn (outside the OneDrive tree, so sync never touches multi-GB files)

container.memory_note: The host has 31.2 GB but WSL2 hands the container 15.2 GB by default. Spark's driver is set to 10 GB, which fits with headroom. No result here should be read as what this CPU can do, only as what this configuration did.

why_docker: The host runs Python 3.14 and Java 25. PySpark 3.5 supports neither. Native Windows Spark also needs winutils hacks and gives unrepresentative timings, so the container pins the combination Spark is actually tested against.

how_to_read_the_benchmarks.spark_opt.json: Median of 5 timed runs per variant after 1 untimed warm-up, all four variants in one Spark session on an identical slice, shuffle partitions held constant. Raw per-run timings are in the artifact. Comparisons are only valid within a session: the same baseline code has measured 40.5s and 50.7s across sessions on the same slice, roughly 25% drift from JIT warmth, page cache and GC state.
how_to_read_the_benchmarks.streaming_lag.json: An accelerated replay from a local file into a single-broker Redpanda on this same laptop. No network hop, no broker cluster, no competing load. It measures this pipeline's processing latency, not a production system's.
how_to_read_the_benchmarks.graph_stats.json: Single-run Cypher timings against a warm page cache. Indicative, not a benchmark. Read them as 'this is not slow', not as 'this is fast'.
how_to_read_the_benchmarks.dataset.json: The row counts are counted, by walking all 7.2 GB. They are not the figures quoted in the LANL paper.
